figures.caption: Table 36: Quantum–classical BA gap at 10% and 100% training fraction (negative = classical better).

Table 35: Learning curve slopes (BA vs. log ntrain) with significance. Positive slopes indicate performance improves with more training data. Parkinson 489 is excluded as it was added in Phase 3 with a different pipeline configuration.
Table 11: Dataset provenance. UCI ID: identifier used by ucimlrepo.fetch ucirepo(); OpenML name: identifier used by sklearn.datasets.fetch openml() as fallback.
Table 6: Wilcoxon signed-rank tests: best quantum (ideal) vs. best classical per dataset. BA: balanced accuracy. ∆: quantum − classical BA. All tests non-significant at α = 0.05.
Figure 2: Kruskal–Wallis epsilon-squared (ε2) effect sizes for seven experimental factors. Dataset choice dominates performance variance at ε2 = 0.73, while kernel family contributes only ε2 = 0.089.

Figure 5: Dataset suitability criterion: non-linearity gap (RBF − linear BA) vs. quantum advantage (∆). Spearman ρ = 0.683 (p = 0.042). Haberman is the only dataset above the dashed parity line, indicating positive quantum advantage.
Table 2: Dataset characteristics. N : post-cleaning sample count; d: original features; Class ratio: majority/minority class balance.
Table 15: ZZFeatureMap circuit properties at R = 2 repetitions.

Table 37: Seed stability (CoV of mean BA across 16 seeds) and paired quantum vs. classical analysis. Parkinson 489 and spambase are excluded: seed analysis was conducted on the seven Phase 1 datasets.
Table 39: Dataset suitability for quantum kernels. N : post-cleaning sample count (identical to Table 2). Non-linearity gap = BArbf − BAlinear; ∆ = BAQ − BAC.
Table 38: QKT optimisation stability summary across 28 configurations.

Figure 4: Kernel matrix heatmaps for HW-01 (belis, breast cancer, k=10). Left to right: ideal statevector simulation, depolarising noise simulation, and IBM ibm fez hardware. The belis kernel is near-identity with off-diagonal values ≈ 0.045; the hardware reproduces this structure with Pearson r = 0.986.

Figure 3: Kernel mean absolute error (MAE) versus transpiled circuit depth for all six hardware experiments. Colour encodes the feature map (blue: belis, pink: rot2dof); marker shape encodes the dataset (circle: breast cancer, square: sonar, diamond: diabetes pima). Deeper circuits (belis, depth ≥ 62) incur higher MAE than shallow circuits (rot2dof, depth 22), confirming that circuit depth is the primary driver of hardware noise.
Table 5: Best balanced accuracy (BA) per dataset. Q-Ideal: best quantum kernel (ideal simulation); Q-QKT: best quantum kernel with QKT; Classical: best classical baseline. ∆: Q-Ideal − Classical.

Figure 1: Balanced accuracy comparison across all nine datasets. For each dataset, bars show the best classical kernel (blue), best quantum ideal kernel (orange), and best quantum kernel with QKT (purple, where available). Classical kernels dominate on 8/9 datasets; haberman is the sole exception.

Table 30: Pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests: best quantum ideal vs. best classical, per dataset. All tests use 5 paired observations (outer folds). This table shows the 9 best-vsbest comparisons; the full set of 29 pairwise comparisons (multiple quantum–classical pairs per dataset) is available in the supplementary data.
Table 4: Hardware experiments on IBM ibm fez (Heron r2). All use 1 024 shots, k = 6−10 features, and nsub = 60 stratified subsample.

Figure 6: Computational cost comparison (log scale). Classical kernels complete in ∼0.31 s, quantum statevector simulation requires ∼3.1 s (10×), and quantum kernel training (QKT) requires ∼635 s (2,060×).

Table 40: Learning curve slopes (BA vs. log ntrain) and gap evolution. Slopes are computed via OLS regression across six training fractions. Quantum ideal slopes are steeper on 6/8 datasets. “Gap narrows” indicates the quantum–classical gap decreases from 10% to 100% training data.
Table 31: Friedman test results within each dataset (all quantum + classical configs). All datasets show significant within-method variation (p < 0.001).
Table 9: Seed sensitivity: coefficient of variation (CoV) of mean balanced accuracy (BA) across 16 seeds. “QI Wins”: number of seeds where quantum ideal BA exceeds classical BA.

Table 8: Hardware validation results on IBM ibm fez (Heron r2). FM: feature map. HW: hardware. r: Pearson correlation between hardware and ideal kernel matrices (off-diagonal entries). BA: downstream 5-fold SVM balanced accuracy on n = 60 subsample. ∆: hardware − ideal.
Table 29: Summary of all six hardware experiments on ibm fez.

Figure 7: Learning curves for all eight datasets across six training fractions (10%–100%). Each panel shows classical (blue), quantum ideal (orange), and quantum noisy (magenta) balanced accuracy as a function of training set size. Quantum kernels show steeper slopes on 6/8 datasets but fail to close the gap.
Table 7: Kernel matrix spectral properties across extended-study configurations (n = 95 kernel matrices, 3 datasets). Effective rank ratio normalised by matrix size N .
